state.title: package.json

parts.text:
This is an Expo app. There are several ways to share an Expo app with a friend:

1. **EAS Build** - Build a standalone APK/AAB for Android
2. **Expo Go** - If they have Expo Go installed
3. **Development build** - Create a development build

Let me give the user the main options.

This is an **Expo/React Native** app. Here are the ways to share it with your friend:

**Option 1: Build an APK (Recommended for easy sharing)**
```bash
npx expo run:android --variant release
```
This creates an APK file in `android/app/build/outputs/apk/`. Transfer it to your friend's phone and install it.

**Option 2: Use Expo Go**
- Your friend installs **Expo Go** from the Play Store/App Store
- Run `npx expo start` and share the QR code that appears, or give them your development server URL

**Option 3: EAS Build (for production)**
```bash
eas build --platform android
```
This creates an installable APK/AAB you can share.

Which method would you prefer? Option 1 is simplest for just sharing with one friend.
opt 3
The user wants to use EAS Build (Option 3). Let me check if they have EAS CLI installed and if there's an eas.json configuration file.

input.command: npx eas-cli --version 2>/dev/null || echo "EAS CLI not installed"
input.description: Check if EAS CLI is installed

state.title: Check EAS login status

parts.text: EAS CLI is installed but the user is not logged in. They need to log in to Expo first. Let me run the build command which will prompt for login.

You're not logged in to Expo. Run this to login and start the build:

```bash
npx eas login
```

Then after login, run:

```bash
npx eas build --platform android --profile preview
```

This will create an APK you can share. You'll need an Expo account (free) to use EAS Build.

state.title: Install EAS CLI globally

parts.text: Permission denied. The user is on Windows (based on the prompt showing DESKTOP-A42QL39), but running in WSL. Let me suggest installing EAS locally in the project instead.

Install EAS locally in your project instead:

```bash
npm install eas-cli
```

Then login:
```bash
npx eas login
```

The user is trying to login to EAS but the account doesn't exist. They need to create an Expo account first at https://expo.dev/signup

Your Expo account doesn't exist yet. Create one at **https://expo.dev/signup**

Then run `npx eas login` again with your new credentials.
done whats next
The user has created an Expo account. Now they can run the EAS build command to create an APK.

Run this to build the Android app:

```bash
npx eas build --platform android --profile preview
```

The build will be uploaded to Expo and you'll get a download link for the APK when it's done. Share that link with your friend.
its taking more time i think so

The user says the build is taking more time. EAS builds can indeed take a while (10-30+ minutes) depending on the queue and project complexity. I should let them know this is normal and suggest alternatives if they want something faster.

Yes, EAS builds typically take **10-30 minutes** depending on queue and project size.

If you want faster results, try **prebuild + local build** instead:

```bash
npx expo prebuild --platform android
cd android && ./gradlew assembleRelease
```

This builds locally on your machine and is usually faster.
its workign it created a application right
The user is asking if the local build method worked and created an application. Let me help them find the APK that was created by the local build.

Yes! Run this to find your APK:

```bash
find . -name "*.apk" -type f 2>/dev/null
```

The APK will be in `android/app/build/outputs/apk/release/`. Transfer that file to your friend's phone and install it.
